notes: CRY aloud (Heb. garown) "with the throat," that is, with full voice, not merely from the lips

afflicted our soul. Some have viewed fasting as a time to seek suffering or self- abasement (Lev. 16:29, 31; 23:27, 32). In 58:5, the Lord rebukes such attitudes, saying he has not commanded a fast wherein a person would "afflict his soul." (Donald W. Parry, Jay A. Parry, and Tina M. Peterson,
Wherefore have we fasted, [say they], and thou seest not? ["Why is it that, when we fast, Thou dost not notice it" (by delivering us)?]
Fast from the German fasten; Dutch vast, firm; vasten, to fast; Swedish fasta; from the same root as fast, firm.

Day of Atonement was the only festival that they celebrated by fasting. All the rest were feasts. But, not only is it the Day of Atonement, but it's the day of atonement in the Year of Jubilee. And the Year of Jubilee is symbolic of having entered into the rest of the Lord, or the Millennial Reign. So it was 49 plus 1, it was the 50th year. It is the ultimate release from bondage. It is when all lands of inheritance went back to the original owner. It's when anybody that was endentured as a servant, was free. It was when the land sat fallow. It is when Israel celebrated their release from bondage. Because the ultimate release from bondage is entering into the presence of the Lord. So, on the day of fast, you don't eat anything from the land. It's a symbolic gesture of letting the land set free. What Isaiah is explaining is, that when you fast, what you are doing is regenerating the Year of Jubilee, and the Day of Atonement in your life.

Bishop Vandenberg also equated these verses with the two great commandments to love God and our neighbor: I suppose when he speaks of "loosing the bands of wickedness" of "undoing the heavy burdens," and the "breaking of every yoke" that he is referring to the wickedness of people who think only of themselves in selfishness, vanity, pride, and having hearts so set upon the things of this world that the two great commandments of loving God and loving neighbor are entirely forgotten. The principles of loving thy neighbor and of loving God are encompassed in the true purpose of the fast. Certainly, it takes no imagination to understand what is meant when he says, "... that thou bring the

You have the Sabbath day, which celebrates the same thing, but you can recreate the Sabbath day in your life, by having a personal fast. So fasting is recreating the millennium, just as the Sabbath day recreates the millennium, or every 7th day is a mini millennium, but if you need extra help for your voice to be heard on high, what can you do? Do a proper fast.
undo the heavy burdens. These burdens might be those a master would place on a servant, or they

quotes.text: Combining spiritual, social, physical, and family responsibilities, verses 6 and 7 summarize the service the people of God should render each other. These verses parallel each other both in form and theme: grammatically, they both open with a question; structurally, they follow a pattern of composite parallelism in developing similar concepts about fasting. Verse 6 stresses spiritual and social responsibilities, and verse 7 emphasizes temporal and familial opportunities for service. The climax of each verse is a phrase that includes any other service not expressly mentioned: "break every yoke" and do not "turn away from your own flesh and blood." The parallelisms are a bit more obvious in the original Hebrew, but they can still be outlined in English as follows: Israel's spiritual responsibilities (v. 6): A. To loose the chains of injustice (or wickedness, KJV) A'. To untie the yoke bonds (of sin) A". and free the oppressed (from social and Satanic persecution) C. and break every yoke Israel's physical responsibilities (v. 7): B. To feed the hungry B'. and provide for the homeless B". To cloth the naked C'. and fulfill duties for kin (or mankind) To review, reread verses 6 and 7 in either the King James Version or the New International Version and note the grammatical pattern in the verses. Each begins with an interrogative and concludes with a summary phrase. Each verse follows a composite pattern of semantic parallelism in describing at least three ways in which the fast should be observed. Careful study will reveal other forms of semantic parallelism in this set of verses: synonymous, since verses 6 and 7 present parallel ideas on the same theme; synthetic, since a question-answer format is followed; and climactic, since both verses build to verse 8, which describes verses 6 and 7. Studying Isaiah's poetic style should not distract from the beautiful teachings found in these verses, but should enhance them. These verses inspire the reader through style as well as content.
quotes.speaker: Victor L. Ludlow
quotes.attr: Victor L. Ludlow, Isaiah: Prophet, Seer, and Poet, p.486

notes: bread to the hungry/bring the poor/cover him. These actions are the essence of pure religion (Matt. 25:31-46; James 1:27). Those who have done these things will receive God's blessings (Ezek. 18:7-9,

Isaiah has a promise of health in proper fasting. Think about it. Many sicknesses are psychosomatic, because we hold grudges, or are angry or fearful and so forth. A proper fast is making everything right. So that even psychosomatic illnesses or emotional problems will begin to dissipate if you fast properly, because you are making everything right around you. You are using fasting as a Sabbath day. And, remember, what is the millennium for? To make all of your final preparations, enter into the celestial kingdom. What is a fast being used for? The Day of Atonement and the year of Jubilee to make all of your final preparations to enter into the presence of the Lord by making everything right and peaceful around you.

When an army was marching, you always took the weak in the back and the sick or the wounded, therefore, an attacking army would often attack from the back. So, you had to put some good troops in the front, and some good troops in the back. And the back troops were called your guard, or your rearward. So, your most vulnerable parts, he says out in front of you is your righteousness, who is protecting your back side and your weak part? He says, I'll do that. I'll look after the weak part of you. It's a promise for doing a correct fast.

the putting forth of the finger [gesture of insult (Prov. 6:13)] Finding fault with others, which is the putting forth of the finger, and lifting yourself up in vanity, you've got to do away with those, in a proper fast.

How much darkness is there at noonday? This is a cryptic scriptural way of making your calling and election sure. You have chased all darkness from you, therefore, you are worthy of calling and election,
and as the Savior explained in section 88 of the Doctrine and Covenants, when you've chased all darkness from you, He says, then you can see me face to face.

the Lord shall guide thee/satisfy thy soul in drought/make fat thy bones. As a shepherd, the Lord leads his flock (which also includes us in the latter days), providing for their every need (Ps. 23:2-3). For those who become his sheep, he provides living water in times of spiritual drought. Bones were considered the gauge of the body's vitality-they become dry and brittle with age and illness but are moist and fresh with youth, health, and vigor (Job 21:24). Bones that are made fat are renewed and made strong (66:14). Make fat thy bones refers to the resurrection.
Notice, he is explaining that person becomes just like the Garden of Eden. Because what are you living in the Year of Jubilee; the real Jubilee. You've entered into the real Garden of Eden.
Remember Elijah the prophet who is noted for fasting 40 days and 40 nights? Yet when the drought came, what happened? He ate. He drank. He was taken care of.
The repairer of the breach, The restorer of paths to dwell in [New English Bible "You shall be called Rebuilder of broken walls, Restorer of houses in ruins."].
If thou turn away thy foot from the sabbath, [from] doing thy pleasure on my holy day [NIV: "If you keep your feet from breaking the Sabbath and doing as you please on my holy day."]
